threats.title: Protocol Halt via calculate_rebase Underflow from smNFT Double Counting
threats.hypothesis_description: The `calculate_rebase` function in `MinterUpgradeable` double-counts the `smNFTBalance`, which can cause an arithmetic underflow that permanently blocks the `update_period` function and halts all protocol emissions.
threats.investigation_rationale: The `_veTotal` variable reads the total BLACK balance of the `_ve` contract, which already includes the tokens locked in smNFTs. The function then adds `_smNFTBalance` again to calculate `veBlackSupply`. If the smNFT balance is large enough, `veBlackSupply` will exceed `blackSupply`, causing the subtraction `circulatingBlack = blackSupply - veBlackSupply` to underflow and revert.

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Double counting of balance leading to underflow
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: circulatingBlack subtraction will revert on underflow in Solidity 0.8+
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No check to ensure veBlackSupply <= blackSupply

analysis_questions: Does the _ve contract's BLACK balance include smNFT locked tokens?
Can the smNFT balance grow large enough to trigger the underflow?
validation_requirements: Verify IVotingEscrow token holding mechanics
Calculate the exact threshold where underflow occurs

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: As users lock more tokens into smNFTs, the double-counted balance grows. Once the threshold is crossed, any call to `update_period` reverts, permanently freezing the epoch transition and all emissions.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Complete halt of protocol emissions and epoch transitions
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires contract upgrade to fix the formula

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing edge case tests for large smNFT balances

technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: Depends on total supply and smNFT ratio
technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Any time after threshold is reached
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Easily detectable via reverted update_period transactions

threats.title: Loss of Reward Rate via Variable Shadowing in GaugeCL
threats.hypothesis_description: The `notifyRewardAmount` function in `GaugeCL` declares `rewardRate` as a return variable, which shadows the state variable of the same name. As a result, the state variable is never updated, breaking the reward rollover logic and returning 0 for `rewardForDuration`.
threats.investigation_rationale: In Solidity, declaring a return variable with the same name as a state variable causes all assignments within the function to write to the local return variable instead of storage. The `rewardRate` state variable remains 0, meaning `leftover` rewards are always calculated as 0, and external contracts reading `rewardRate` receive incorrect data.

proof_of_signal.pattern: returns (..., uint256 rewardRate, ...) shadows uint256 public rewardRate;

preconditions: notifyRewardAmount is called to distribute rewards

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: State variable shadowing in return declaration
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: State variable rewardRate is never updated

analysis_questions: Is the state variable rewardRate used anywhere else?
Does the shadowing affect the Algebra virtual pool rate setting?
validation_requirements: Check if leftover calculation evaluates to 0
Verify rewardForDuration returns 0

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: When `notifyRewardAmount` is called before the period finishes, the `leftover` calculation uses the uninitialized local `rewardRate` (0), causing previously undistributed rewards to be permanently lost from the rate calculation.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Loss of leftover rewards during rate updates and incorrect view function returns
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Lost rewards cannot be recovered into the rate

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests verifying rewardRate state updates

threats.title: Permanent Lock of Bribe Rewards via Unbounded Loop DoS
threats.hypothesis_description: The `earned` function in `Bribes.sol` iterates over every epoch since the user's last claim. With 30-minute epochs, this loop will exceed the block gas limit if a user does not claim for an extended period, permanently locking their rewards.
threats.investigation_rationale: The `WEEK` constant is set to 1800 seconds (30 minutes), resulting in 48 epochs per day. If a user waits 30 days to claim, the loop runs 1440 times, performing multiple state reads (SLOADs) and binary searches per iteration. This will easily exceed the Avalanche block gas limit, causing `getReward` to revert unconditionally.
deployment_assumptions: WEEK is configured to 1800 seconds in production
threats.config_evidence: BlackTimeLibrary.sol line 6: uint256 internal constant WEEK = 1800;

proof_of_signal.pattern: for (uint256 i = 0; i < numEpochs; i++) with state reads inside

preconditions: User does not claim rewards for a large number of epochs

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Gas cost scales linearly with time since last claim
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No maximum loop bound or partial claim mechanism

analysis_questions: What is the exact gas cost per iteration?
How many days of inactivity trigger the DoS?
validation_requirements: Simulate getReward after 30 days of inactivity
Measure gas consumption per epoch

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A user deposits votes and forgets to claim bribes for a month. When they return and call `claimBribes`, the transaction reverts due to out-of-gas. The user's rewards are permanently locked with no way to partially claim.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Permanent loss of user bribe rewards

technical_investigation_focus.gas_analysis_needed: Calculate iterations to hit 8M/15M gas limit

threats.title: Bribe Insolvency via Incorrect Recovery Function Call
threats.hypothesis_description: The `recoverERC20AndUpdateData` function in `BribeFactoryV3` calls `emergencyRecoverERC20` on the Bribe contract instead of `recoverERC20AndUpdateData`, failing to update internal reward accounting and leaving the Bribe contract insolvent.
threats.investigation_rationale: When the admin attempts to recover tokens and update the data, the factory mistakenly calls the emergency function which only transfers the tokens out. The `tokenRewardsPerEpoch` mapping in the Bribe contract remains inflated. Users will attempt to claim rewards based on this inflated accounting, but the contract will lack the token balance to fulfill the transfers, causing reverts for legitimate claimants.
preconditions: Admin calls recoverERC20AndUpdateData on the factory

analysis_questions: Does the Bribe contract have a recoverERC20AndUpdateData function?
What happens to users who try to claim after recovery?
validation_requirements: Trace the state of tokenRewardsPerEpoch after factory call
Verify insolvency during user claims

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Admin calls `recoverERC20AndUpdateData` to fix a token distribution error. The tokens are withdrawn, but the Bribe's accounting still shows them as available. When users claim, the first few succeed until the contract is drained, and subsequent legitimate users cannot claim their earned rewards.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Insolvency of Bribe contract and loss of user rewards

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing integration tests for factory recovery functions

threats.title: Governance Broken via Empty Clock Implementations
threats.hypothesis_description: The `BlackGovernor` contract implements `clock()` and `CLOCK_MODE()` as empty functions returning default values (0 and empty string). This completely breaks the OpenZeppelin Governor snapshot and voting mechanics.
threats.investigation_rationale: OpenZeppelin's `Governor` relies on `clock()` to determine the current timepoint for proposal snapshots and deadlines. Because `clock()` returns 0, all proposals will have a snapshot timepoint of 0. When votes are cast, the governor will query historical voting power at timepoint 0, which will always be 0. No proposal can ever pass.

proof_of_signal.pattern: function clock() public view override returns (uint48) {}

preconditions: A proposal is created and users attempt to vote

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Returns default 0 instead of block.timestamp or block.number

analysis_questions: How does the inherited Governor contract handle a 0 snapshot?
Can any votes be successfully counted?
validation_requirements: Deploy BlackGovernor and create a proposal
Attempt to vote and check if voting power is recognized

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A legitimate governance proposal is created to nudge the emission rate. Users attempt to vote, but their voting power is evaluated at timepoint 0 (before they locked tokens). All votes have 0 weight, and the proposal fails to reach quorum.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Complete failure of the governance system
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires deploying a new Governor contract

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Visible via 0 voting power on all votes

threats.title: Governance Quorum Manipulation via Current Supply Lookup
threats.hypothesis_description: The `quorum` function in `BlackGovernor` calculates the required quorum using the current total supply of smNFTs rather than the historical supply at the proposal's snapshot timepoint, allowing attackers to manipulate the quorum during the voting period.
threats.investigation_rationale: The function calls `token.getsmNFTPastTotalSupply()` without passing the `blockTimestamp` parameter. Looking at `IBlackHoleVotes`, this function takes no parameters and returns the current supply. An attacker can mint or burn smNFTs while a proposal is active to artificially raise or lower the quorum threshold, ensuring malicious proposals pass or legitimate ones fail.
preconditions: smNFTs can be minted or burned during a voting period

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Using current state for historical quorum calculation
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Violates Governor standard which requires snapshot evaluation
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No timestamp parameter passed to supply lookup

analysis_questions: Can smNFTs be minted/burned freely by users?
How much does the quorum change per smNFT?
validation_requirements: Check smNFT minting mechanics
Simulate quorum change during active proposal

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: An attacker creates a malicious proposal. To ensure it passes with minimal votes, they burn their smNFTs (or wait for others to decay/burn) to lower the current total supply, dropping the quorum threshold just before the proposal deadline.
threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for quorum stability during voting

threats.title: BribeFactoryV3 addRewardsToBribes Out-of-Bounds Revert
threats.hypothesis_description: The `addRewardsToBribes` function in `BribeFactoryV3` contains an indexing bug in its inner loop, using the length of the outer array instead of the inner array, causing it to revert with an out-of-bounds error for most inputs.
threats.investigation_rationale: The inner loop iterates `k` from 0 to `_token.length` (the number of bribes) but accesses `_token[i][k]`. If the inner array `_token[i]` has fewer elements than the outer array `_token`, the access `_token[i][k]` will throw an index out-of-bounds exception, rendering the batch function unusable.
preconditions: Admin calls addRewardsToBribes with inner arrays shorter than the outer array

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Incorrect array length used in nested loop

analysis_questions: Is this function critical for protocol operations?
Can admins use single-bribe functions as a workaround?
validation_requirements: Test function with mismatched array lengths

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Admin attempts to batch add 1 reward token to 5 different bribes. `_token.length` is 5, but `_token[0].length` is 1. When `k=1`, `_token[0][1]` throws an out-of-bounds error, reverting the transaction.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Operational friction, no direct fund loss

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for batch reward addition

threats.title: GaugeManager _setExternalBribe Emits Incorrect Old Address
threats.hypothesis_description: The `_setExternalBribe` function in `GaugeManager` emits the `internal_bribes` address instead of the `external_bribes` address as the old value in the `SetBribeFor` event, corrupting off-chain tracking.
threats.investigation_rationale: The event emission `emit SetBribeFor(false, internal_bribes[_gauge], _external, _gauge);` uses a copy-pasted variable from `_setInternalBribe`. This causes off-chain indexers, governance dashboards, and audit trails to record the wrong historical external bribe address, which can obscure malicious admin actions or break UI integrations.

analysis_questions: Do off-chain systems rely on this event for critical logic?
Does this obscure admin accountability?
validation_requirements: Verify event logs after calling setExternalBribeFor

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A compromised admin changes the external bribe to a malicious contract. Auditors reviewing the event logs see the internal bribe address as the 'old' address, creating confusion and delaying incident response.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: None directly, but hinders monitoring and incident response

threats.title: EpochController performUpkeep Reverts on Uninitialized genesisManager
threats.hypothesis_description: The `EpochController` does not set `genesisManager` in its `initialize` function. If `performUpkeep` is called before `setGenesisManager` is manually invoked, the transaction reverts, blocking epoch transitions and protocol emissions.
threats.investigation_rationale: The `performUpkeep` function calls `genesisManager.checkAtEpochFlip()`. Because `genesisManager` is address(0) upon initialization, this external call reverts. This is a critical configuration gap that will break the core epoch automation if the deployment script misses the post-deploy setter call.
deployment_assumptions: setGenesisManager is not called before the first epoch flip

proof_of_signal.pattern: genesisManager.checkAtEpochFlip(); called on potentially zero address

preconditions: performUpkeep is triggered before setGenesisManager is called

analysis_questions: Do the deployment scripts reliably call setGenesisManager?
Is there a fallback mechanism to trigger epoch flips?
validation_requirements: Review deployment scripts for setGenesisManager calls
Test performUpkeep with genesisManager = address(0)

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: The protocol launches and the first epoch ends. Chainlink automation triggers `performUpkeep`, but it reverts because `genesisManager` is 0x0. Emissions are halted until the admin realizes the issue and calls `setGenesisManager`.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Delay in protocol emissions and fee distributions

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for uninitialized state behavior

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Visible via reverted Chainlink upkeep transactions

threats.title: Permanent Loss of Emissions via notifyRewardAmount with Zero Weight
threats.hypothesis_description: If `notifyRewardAmount` in `GaugeManager` is called when `totalWeight` is 0, the transferred emissions are permanently locked in the contract because the global reward index is not updated.
threats.investigation_rationale: The function transfers `amount` of `base` tokens from the minter to the `GaugeManager`. It then calculates `_ratio = amount * 1e18 / Math.max(totalWeight, 1)`. If `totalWeight == 0`, `_ratio` is 0, and `index` is not incremented. Because gauge claimable amounts rely on `index` increasing, these tokens can never be distributed or recovered.

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: State update skipped after token transfer
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Tokens are trapped without a recovery mechanism
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No refund or delayed distribution logic for zero weight

analysis_questions: Can totalWeight be 0 during normal operations (e.g., at launch)?
Is there any sweep function in GaugeManager?
validation_requirements: Simulate epoch flip with 0 votes
Verify tokens are stuck in GaugeManager

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: At protocol launch, before any users have locked tokens and voted, the first epoch flip occurs. Emissions are minted and sent to `GaugeManager`, but because `totalWeight` is 0, the index doesn't move. The first week's emissions are permanently burned.

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Visible via trapped BLACK tokens in GaugeManager

threats.title: Voter and Bribe Desync via Silent withdraw Failure
threats.hypothesis_description: The `withdraw` function in `Bribes.sol` silently fails to update state if the requested `amount` exceeds `balanceOf[tokenId]`. This causes a permanent desynchronization between the Voter contract's tracked weights and the Bribe contract's actual balances.
threats.investigation_rationale: The `if (amount <= balanceOf[tokenId])` guard prevents underflows but does not revert if the condition fails. If the Voter contract calls `withdraw` with an amount slightly higher than the Bribe's balance (e.g., due to rounding differences or other bugs), the Bribe retains the user's full balance. The Voter considers the vote reset, but the user continues to earn bribe rewards indefinitely.
preconditions: Voter calls withdraw with amount > balanceOf[tokenId]

initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Should revert or withdraw max available

analysis_questions: Under what conditions can Voter's tracked weight exceed Bribe's balance?
Does the AVM or smNFT logic create rounding discrepancies?
validation_requirements: Analyze Voter.reset() weight calculations
Test withdraw with amount > balance

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A user manipulates their veNFT balance such that the Voter calculates a reset weight of X+1, while the Bribe only has X. The `withdraw` call silently fails. The user's votes are freed in the Voter, but remain active in the Bribe, allowing them to double-vote or earn infinite bribes.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Theft of bribe rewards from legitimate voters
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Other voters in the same gauge

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for desynced withdrawal amounts

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Visible via totalSupply > sum of active votes

threats.title: Fee Distribution DoS via Unbounded Referral Fee
threats.hypothesis_description: The `setReferralFee` function in `GaugeFactoryCL` lacks an upper bound check. If set to a value greater than 1000 (100%), the `_claimFees` function in `GaugeCL` will underflow, permanently blocking fee distribution for all CL gauges.
threats.investigation_rationale: In `GaugeCL._claimFees`, the dibs fee is calculated as `claimed0 * referralFee / REFERRAL_FEE_DENOMINATOR`. If `referralFee` > 1000, `_dibsFeeToken0` will be greater than `claimed0`. The subsequent subtraction `claimed0 -= _dibsFeeToken0` will underflow and revert. This blocks the entire `distributeFees` process if called via the loop.

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Missing bounds check on fee parameter

analysis_questions: Can a compromised admin use this to hold the protocol hostage?
Does this revert block the entire distributeFees loop?
validation_requirements: Test setReferralFee with 1001
Verify _claimFees reverts

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A compromised or malicious admin calls `setReferralFee(2000)`. When `GaugeManager.distributeFees()` is called, it iterates over all pools. When it hits a CL gauge, `_claimFees` underflows and reverts the entire transaction, halting all fee distributions across the protocol.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Denial of service for LP fee claims
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Admin must reset the fee to <= 1000

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing bounds validation tests for fee setters
